humanities_role_explanation: Uses historical library archives (e.g., Sherardian Library of botanists), geographical trivia, and media/history queries as content for complex multi-step web-search and collation tasks.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only prompts requiring agents to query and retrieve text-based information from the open web.
analytical_tasks_explanation: The benchmark operationalizes systematic collation of fragmented web information, entity de-duplication across diverse source texts, multi-hop constraint solving (e.g., intersecting attributes like populations, GDP, and years), and autonomous determination of search completion under epistemic uncertainty.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates agents on general-purpose deep research tasks across 17 diverse domain profiles (politics, science, health, history, geography, finance, etc.).

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates Gemini Deep Research, GPT-5 Pro High Reasoning, GPT-5 High Reasoning, Gemini 3 Pro Preview, o3 Deep Research, o4 Mini Deep Research, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude 4.5 family.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLM-based autonomous web agents are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes history (e.g., historical library records and archives) and geography/media topics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses F1-Score, Precision, Recall, and categorical classification (Fully Correct, Fully Incorrect, Partially Correct, Correct with Extraneous Answers).

english_language.note: The paper and benchmark dataset are written in English.

summary: DeepSearchQA introduces a 900-prompt dataset to benchmark autonomous web agents on challenging multi-step information-seeking tasks requiring exhaustive answer-set generation rather than single-answer retrieval. The evaluation measures how effectively agents handle systematic collation of fragmented data, entity resolution, and stopping criteria. The results show a clear gap between advanced deep-research agents and standard reasoning models.
key_findings: Gemini Deep Research Agent and GPT-5 Pro High Reasoning establish the state-of-the-art (F1 scores of 81.90% and 78.98% respectively). Performance drops significantly for smaller models (e.g., Gemini 2.5 Flash drops to 42.99% F1 and 45.27% fully incorrect), illustrating a hard reasoning threshold for agentic search. Increasing test-time compute via multiple samples monotonically scales accuracy (Gemini Deep Research climbs from 67.18% fully correct at n=1 to 85.71% at n=8).
